Music and the Mind

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

PSY 120L Reproducible Analysis Report

Author

Sophia Martins

Published

June 4, 2026

About This Report

This report documents the complete reproducible workflow used to analyze the effects of a brief music prime on selective attention and affective state. All data preparation, scale construction, descriptive statistics, reliability analyses, ANOVAs, and figures were generated directly from the source dataset using R and Quarto.

Study Overview

The current study examined whether a brief, moderately arousing instrumental music prime influenced selective attention and affective state. Participants were randomly assigned to either a music condition or a silence control condition. Selective attention was assessed using the Flanker Task and affective state was measured using the PANAS. Music reward sensitivity was measured using the BMRQ and examined as a potential moderator.

Setup and Software Environment

This report was created using R, RStudio, and Quarto. All data preparation, statistical analyses, tables, and figures were generated directly from code to ensure a fully reproducible workflow. The analyses relied on packages from the tidyverse ecosystem for data management and visualization, psych for reliability analyses, and rstatix and emmeans for statistical testing and follow-up comparisons. The software environment information below provides the package versions and system details necessary to reproduce the results.

Load Packages

The following packages were loaded to support data management, visualization, reliability analyses, statistical testing, and estimated marginal means calculations used throughout the report.

Show code 
library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals

Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The data analyzed in this report were collected through an online Qualtrics survey designed to examine the effects of a brief music prime on selective attention and affective state. Participants completed the Flanker Task, the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS), and the Barcelona Music Reward Questionnaire (BMRQ), along with demographic questions. The raw dataset was exported from Qualtrics and imported into R for cleaning, variable preparation, statistical analysis, and visualization.

Show code 
study_raw <- read_csv("data/music_mind_data.csv")

Check Imported Dataset

Clean Flanker Item

Show code 
study_raw <- study_raw %>%
  mutate(
    FlankerEffect = str_remove(FlankerEffect, "ms"),
    FlankerEffect = as.numeric(FlankerEffect),
    FlankerEffect = if_else(
      abs(FlankerEffect) > 500,
      NA_real_,
      FlankerEffect
    )
  )

study_raw %>%
  summarise(
    mean_flanker = mean(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_flanker = sd(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_flanker = min(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_flanker = max(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    missing_flanker = sum(is.na(FlankerEffect))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
mean_flanker sd_flanker min_flanker max_flanker missing_flanker
27.8 78.8895 -162 199 39

The Flanker Effect variable was cleaned before creating the analysis dataset. Some values were imported with the text "ms" attached, so this text was removed and the variable was converted to numeric. Extremely large Flanker Effect values were then recoded as missing because they were outside the plausible range for this task and would disproportionately influence the mean, standard deviation, ANOVA results, and figure. After cleaning, the usable Flanker Effect scores ranged from -162 to 199, with 38 missing values. This means the Flanker analyses use only participants with valid, interpretable Flanker Effect scores.

Create Analysis Dataset

Show code 
study_vars <- c(
  
      "gender", "age", "condition",
      
      "Compatible Score",
      "Incongruent Score",
      "FlankerEffect",

      "emo3", "emo8", "emo12", "emo18",
      "seek2", "seek7", "seek11", "seek17",
      "sens5", "sens10", "sens15", "sens20",
      "soc1", "soc6", "soc13", "soc16",
      "mood4", "mood9", "mood14", "mood19",

      "pos1", "pos3", "pos5", "pos9", "pos10",
      "pos14", "pos16", "pos17", "pos19",

      "neg2", "neg4", "neg6", "neg7", "neg8", "neg11", 
      "neg12", "neg13", "neg15", "neg18", "neg20"

      )

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(
    across(everything(), as.numeric)
  )

Check Analysis Dataset

The output confirms that the final analysis dataset contains the variables required for the study, including condition assignment, Flanker Task scores, PANAS items, BMRQ items, and participant identifiers.

Variable Preparation

Several variables were prepared before analysis. PANAS and BMRQ items were scored to create composite measures of positive affect, negative affect, and music reward sensitivity. Variables were recoded as needed, and a median split was used to classify participants into high and low music reward sensitivity groups for the 2 × 2 ANOVA analyses.

Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Control", "Arousal")
    ))

The output confirms that a unique participant identifier and the experimental condition variable were successfully added to the analysis dataset.

Check Experimental Condition Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n")
  )
Condition n
Control 47
Arousal 47
NA 5

The output confirms that the experimental condition variable was coded correctly into the Control and Arousal groups. Participants were distributed evenly across conditions (n = 47 per group), with a small number of cases containing missing condition data that were handled during subsequent analyses.

Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_data)
[1] 99 47

 [1] "gender"            "age"               "condition"        
 [4] "Compatible Score"  "Incongruent Score" "FlankerEffect"    
 [7] "emo3"              "emo8"              "emo12"            
[10] "emo18"             "seek2"             "seek7"

The output confirms that the analysis dataset was successfully prepared and contains 99 participants and 47 variables. The dataset includes the key variables required for analysis, including experimental condition, Flanker Task performance, PANAS items, BMRQ items, and demographic variables.

Reverse-Code Scale Items

Reverse-coded BMRQ items were recoded so that higher values consistently reflected greater music reward sensitivity across all questionnaire items.

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    
        seek2r = 6 - seek2,
        sens5r = 6 - sens5
    
  )

Check Reverse Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(seek2, seek2r, sens5, sens5r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
seek2 seek2r sens5 sens5r
2 4 1 5
1 5 2 4
1 5 1 5
1 5 2 4
4 2 3 3
3 3 2 4
1 5 1 5
1 5 1 5
2 4 3 3
1 5 1 5

Create Scale Scores

Composite scale scores were created for the BMRQ and PANAS measures by averaging responses across their respective items. These scores provide overall measures of music reward sensitivity, positive affect, and negative affect that were used in subsequent reliability analyses and ANOVAs.

Show code 
bmrq_items <- c(
            "emo3", "emo8", "emo12", "emo18", "seek2r", "seek7", 
            "seek11", "seek17", "sens5r", "sens10", "sens15", 
            "sens20", "soc1", "soc6", "soc13", "soc16","mood4", 
            "mood9", "mood14", "mood19"
          )

positive_affect_items <- c(
            "pos1", "pos3", "pos5", "pos9", "pos10", 
            "pos14", "pos16", "pos17", "pos19"
          )

negative_affect_items <- c(
            "neg2", "neg4", "neg6", "neg7", "neg8", 
            "neg11", "neg12", "neg13", "neg15", "neg18", "neg20"
        )


study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    bmrq_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(bmrq_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    positive_affect = rowMeans(across(all_of(positive_affect_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    negative_affect = rowMeans(across(all_of(negative_affect_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

Check Scale Scores

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(
    bmrq_scale,
    positive_affect,
    negative_affect
  ) %>%
  summary()
   bmrq_scale    positive_affect negative_affect
 Min.   :2.450   Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.000  
 1st Qu.:3.612   1st Qu.:1.694   1st Qu.:1.182  
 Median :4.050   Median :2.333   Median :1.455  
 Mean   :3.935   Mean   :2.375   Mean   :1.620  
 3rd Qu.:4.300   3rd Qu.:2.889   3rd Qu.:1.818  
 Max.   :5.000   Max.   :4.111   Max.   :3.909  
 NA's   :37      NA's   :37      NA's   :37
Show code 
study_data %>%
  summarise(
    bmrq_scale_missing = mean(is.na(bmrq_scale)),
    postive_affect_missing = mean(is.na(positive_affect)),
    negative_affect_missing = mean(is.na(negative_affect))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
bmrq_scale_missing postive_affect_missing negative_affect_missing
0.3737374 0.3737374 0.3737374

The summary statistics indicate that the composite scale scores were successfully created and fall within the expected range for each measure. These results suggest that the scoring procedures were implemented correctly and that the variables are suitable for further analysis.

Create BMRQ Median Split

Show code 
bmrq_median <- median(study_data$bmrq_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

study_data <- study_data %>%
mutate(
    bmrq_msplit = if_else(bmrq_scale <= bmrq_median, "Low", "High"),
    bmrq_msplit = factor(bmrq_msplit, levels = c("Low", "High"))
    )

bmrq_median
[1] 4.05
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(bmrq_msplit) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Commitment Group", "n")
  )
Commitment Group n
Low 34
High 28
NA 37

Check Scale Reliability

Reliability analyses were conducted to evaluate the internal consistency of the BMRQ and PANAS scales. Examining reliability helps determine whether the items within each measure consistently assess the same underlying construct before creating final composite scores for analysis.

Show code 
bmrq_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(bmrq_items))
positive_affect_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(positive_affect_items))
negative_affect_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(negative_affect_items))

bmrq_alpha <- psych::alpha(bmrq_item_df)
bmrq_omega <- psych::omega(bmrq_item_df, plot = FALSE)

positive_affect_alpha <- psych::alpha(positive_affect_item_df)
positive_affect_omega <- psych::omega(positive_affect_item_df, plot = FALSE)

negative_affect_alpha <- psych::alpha(negative_affect_item_df)
negative_affect_omega <- psych::omega(negative_affect_item_df, plot = FALSE)

reliability_table <- tibble(
  scale = c("Music Sensitivity", "Positive Affect", "Negative Affect"),
  alpha = c(
    bmrq_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    positive_affect_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    negative_affect_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    bmrq_omega$omega.tot,
    positive_affect_omega$omega.tot,
    negative_affect_omega$omega.tot
  )
)

reliability_table %>%
  mutate(
    alpha = round(alpha, 2),
    omega = round(omega, 2)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Scale", "Cronbach's alpha", "McDonald's omega")
  )
Scale Cronbach’s alpha McDonald’s omega
Music Sensitivity 0.91 0.94
Positive Affect 0.89 0.92
Negative Affect 0.89 0.92

Reliability analyses indicated excellent internal consistency for the BMRQ (α = .91, ω = .94) and PANAS (α = .89, ω = .92). These findings support the use of the composite scale scores in subsequent analyses.

Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics were calculated to summarize the distributions of the primary study variables before conducting inferential analyses.

Scale Score Descriptives

Show code 
# Descriptive statistics for continuous scale variables
study_data %>%
  select(bmrq_scale, positive_affect, negative_affect, FlankerEffect) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
vars n mean sd median trimmed mad min max range skew kurtosis se
bmrq_scale 1 62 3.94 0.58 4.05 3.97 0.44 2.45 5.00 2.55 -0.68 0.09 0.07
positive_affect 2 62 2.37 0.76 2.33 2.35 0.82 1.00 4.11 3.11 0.18 -0.86 0.10
negative_affect 3 62 1.62 0.62 1.45 1.52 0.40 1.00 3.91 2.91 1.59 2.53 0.08
FlankerEffect 4 60 27.80 78.89 21.50 27.31 65.23 -162.00 199.00 361.00 0.10 -0.26 10.18

The descriptive statistics summarize the distributions of the primary study variables. Participants reported moderate levels of music reward sensitivity and positive affect, relatively low levels of negative affect, and substantial variability in Flanker Effect scores. These values provide an overview of the sample prior to hypothesis testing and indicate that the variables were successfully computed.

Show code 
study_data %>%
  summarise(
    mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    sd_age = round(sd(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    min_age = min(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_age = max(age, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Mean Age", "SD Age", "Minimum Age", "Maximum Age")
  )
Mean Age SD Age Minimum Age Maximum Age
23.77 9.17 18 64
Show code 
study_data %>%
  mutate(
    gender = factor(gender,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
      labels = c(
        "Male",
        "Female",
        "Non-binary / third gender",
        "Prefer not to say"
      )
    )
  )
# A tibble: 99 × 53
   gender   age condition `Compatible Score` `Incongruent Score` FlankerEffect
   <fct>  <dbl> <fct>                  <dbl>               <dbl>         <dbl>
 1 Female    22 Arousal                  802                 899            97

#   pos14 <dbl>, pos16 <dbl>, pos17 <dbl>, pos19 <dbl>, neg2 <dbl>, …
Show code 
study_data %>%
 count(gender) %>%
 mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
 knitr::kable()
gender n percent
1 12 12.1
2 49 49.5
NA 38 38.4

The demographic characteristics describe the composition of the sample. Participants ranged in age from young adulthood to older adulthood, providing a relatively broad age range. The gender distribution indicates the representation of participants across gender categories and provides context for interpreting the study findings.

Correlations Among the DVs

Pearson correlations were conducted to examine the relationships among the primary dependent variables, including Flanker Effect scores, positive affect, and negative affect. Examining these associations provides insight into whether affective states were related to selective attention performance.

Show code 
# Correlations for the main continuous dependent variables
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% 
    select(
      FlankerEffect,
      positive_affect,
      negative_affect
    )
)
Call:psych::corr.test(x = study_data %>% select(FlankerEffect, positive_affect, 
    negative_affect))
Correlation matrix 
                FlankerEffect positive_affect negative_affect
FlankerEffect            1.00            0.07            0.01
positive_affect          0.07            1.00            0.42
negative_affect          0.01            0.42            1.00
Sample Size 
                FlankerEffect positive_affect negative_affect
FlankerEffect              60              60              60
positive_affect            60              62              62
negative_affect            60              62              62
Probability values (Entries above the diagonal are adjusted for multiple tests.) 
                FlankerEffect positive_affect negative_affect
FlankerEffect            0.00               1               1
positive_affect          0.62               0               0
negative_affect          0.93               0               0

 To see confidence intervals of the correlations, print with the short=FALSE option

Positive affect and negative affect were significantly positively correlated, indicating that participants who reported higher levels of positive affect also tended to report higher levels of negative affect. Flanker Effect scores were not significantly associated with either affect measure, suggesting that selective attention performance was largely unrelated to self-reported affective state in the current sample.

Primary Analyses

Three separate 2 × 2 ANOVAs were conducted to examine the effects of music condition (Arousal vs. Control) and music reward sensitivity (High vs. Low) on Flanker Effect scores, positive affect, and negative affect. These analyses were used to evaluate whether the music prime influenced cognitive performance and affective state and whether these effects differed as a function of music reward sensitivity.

Flanker Attention Model

Show code 
a1_flanker <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "FlankerEffect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_flanker
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: FlankerEffect
                 Effect    df     MSE      F  ges p.value
1             condition 1, 56 6039.43 3.65 + .061    .061
2           bmrq_msplit 1, 56 6039.43   0.66 .012    .422
3 condition:bmrq_msplit 1, 56 6039.43   0.09 .002    .770
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

Estimated Means for Flanker Attention

Show code 
emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition)
 condition emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control     7.28 14.4 56    -21.6     36.2
 Arousal    46.17 14.3 56     17.5     74.9

Results are averaged over the levels of: bmrq_msplit 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ bmrq_msplit)
 bmrq_msplit emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low           35.0 13.7 56     7.54     62.4
 High          18.5 15.0 56   -11.66     48.6

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
 condition bmrq_msplit emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control   Low          18.50 20.8 56   -23.11     60.1
 Arousal   Low          51.42 17.8 56    15.71     87.1
 Control   High         -3.93 20.1 56   -44.13     36.3
 Arousal   High         40.92 22.4 56    -4.02     85.9

Confidence level used: 0.95

Positive Mood Model

Show code 
a1_positive_affect <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "positive_affect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_positive_affect
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: positive_affect
                 Effect    df  MSE    F  ges p.value
1             condition 1, 58 0.60 0.72 .012    .401
2           bmrq_msplit 1, 58 0.60 0.44 .008    .508
3 condition:bmrq_msplit 1, 58 0.60 0.93 .016    .339
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

Estimated Means for Positive Mood

Show code 
emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition)

 Arousal     2.47 0.139 58     2.19     2.75

Results are averaged over the levels of: bmrq_msplit 
Confidence level used: 0.95

emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ bmrq_msplit)
 bmrq_msplit emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low           2.32 0.133 58     2.05     2.59
 High          2.45 0.146 58     2.16     2.75

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
 condition bmrq_msplit emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control   Low           2.33 0.199 58     1.93     2.73
 Arousal   Low           2.31 0.177 58     1.96     2.66
 Control   High          2.27 0.199 58     1.88     2.67
 Arousal   High          2.63 0.214 58     2.20     3.06

Confidence level used: 0.95

Negative Mood Model

Show code 
a1_negative_affect <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "negative_affect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_negative_affect
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: negative_affect
                 Effect    df  MSE    F   ges p.value
1             condition 1, 58 0.40 0.08  .001    .778
2           bmrq_msplit 1, 58 0.40 0.00 <.001    .966
3 condition:bmrq_msplit 1, 58 0.40 0.40  .007    .530
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

Estimated Means for Negative Mood

Show code 
emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control     1.65 0.115 58     1.42     1.88
 Arousal     1.60 0.113 58     1.38     1.83

Results are averaged over the levels of: bmrq_msplit 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ bmrq_msplit)
 bmrq_msplit emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low           1.63 0.109 58     1.41     1.85
 High          1.62 0.119 58     1.38     1.86

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
 condition bmrq_msplit emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control   Low           1.70 0.163 58     1.38     2.03
 Arousal   Low           1.56 0.145 58     1.27     1.84
 Control   High          1.59 0.163 58     1.27     1.92
 Arousal   High          1.65 0.175 58     1.30     2.00

Confidence level used: 0.95

Figures

The figure below displays mean Flanker Effect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.Flanker Attention Interaction Plot

Show code 
p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    color = bmrq_msplit,
    group = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_line(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Flanker Effect",
    color = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(-50, 100, 25)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(-50, 100)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar

Participants in the music condition tended to exhibit larger Flanker Effect scores than participants in the control condition, regardless of music reward sensitivity level. The relatively overlapping confidence intervals suggest that these differences were small, consistent with the nonsignificant ANOVA findings.

Positive Affect Interaction Plot

The figure below displays mean positive affect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Show code 
p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Positive Affect",
    fill = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar

The Positive Affect figure shows a slight tendency for participants in the music condition to report higher positive affect than participants in the control condition. However, positive affect scores were generally similar across groups, and the overlapping confidence intervals are consistent with the absence of significant main or interaction effects.

Negative Affect Interaction Plot

The figure below displays mean negative affect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Show code 
p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Negative Affect",
    fill = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar

Negative affect scores remained relatively stable across conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. The substantial overlap in confidence intervals indicates minimal differences between groups, supporting the nonsignificant ANOVA results.

Export Figures

Show code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/flanker_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/positive_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/negative_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

The figures illustrate the pattern of means across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups for each dependent variable. Participants in the music condition tended to show slightly larger Flanker Effect scores and slightly higher positive affect than participants in the control condition, whereas negative affect remained relatively similar across groups. Overall, the figures suggest minimal differences between experimental conditions, consistent with the statistical results.

Results Summary

The present study examined whether a brief music prime influenced selective attention and affective state and whether these effects depended on music reward sensitivity. Overall, the findings did not support the study hypotheses. Participants exposed to the music prime did not demonstrate significantly different levels of selective attention, positive affect, or negative affect compared to participants in the control condition. Additionally, music reward sensitivity did not appear to influence the relationship between the music prime and the outcome variables. Although some descriptive differences were observed between conditions, the overall pattern of results suggests that the brief music prime had limited effects on cognitive performance and affective state in the current sample.

Reproducibility Information

The information below describes the software environment used to create this report. Recording the R version, operating system, and package versions helps ensure that the analyses can be reproduced and verified by other researchers.

Source Code
---
title: "Music and the Mind"
subtitle: "PSY 120L Reproducible Analysis Report"
author: "Sophia Martins"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: lux #you can update this!
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Report Sections"
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: tango
    embed-resources: true
    smooth-scroll: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## About This Report

This report documents the complete reproducible workflow used to analyze the effects of a brief music prime on selective attention and affective state. All data preparation, scale construction, descriptive statistics, reliability analyses, ANOVAs, and figures were generated directly from the source dataset using R and Quarto.

## Study Overview

The current study examined whether a brief, moderately arousing instrumental music prime influenced selective attention and affective state. Participants were randomly assigned to either a music condition or a silence control condition. Selective attention was assessed using the Flanker Task and affective state was measured using the PANAS. Music reward sensitivity was measured using the BMRQ and examined as a potential moderator.

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)

options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Setup and Software Environment

This report was created using R, RStudio, and Quarto. All data preparation, statistical analyses, tables, and figures were generated directly from code to ensure a fully reproducible workflow. The analyses relied on packages from the tidyverse ecosystem for data management and visualization, psych for reliability analyses, and rstatix and emmeans for statistical testing and follow-up comparisons. The software environment information below provides the package versions and system details necessary to reproduce the results.

**Load Packages**

The following packages were loaded to support data management, visualization, reliability analyses, statistical testing, and estimated marginal means calculations used throughout the report.

```{r}
#| label: load_packages
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The data analyzed in this report were collected through an online Qualtrics survey designed to examine the effects of a brief music prime on selective attention and affective state. Participants completed the Flanker Task, the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS), and the Barcelona Music Reward Questionnaire (BMRQ), along with demographic questions. The raw dataset was exported from Qualtrics and imported into R for cleaning, variable preparation, statistical analysis, and visualization.

```{r}
#| label: import_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_raw <- read_csv("data/music_mind_data.csv")
```

### Check Imported Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_imported_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_raw)

names(study_raw)

head(study_raw)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Clean Flanker Item

```{r}
#| label: clean-flanker-item
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_raw <- study_raw %>%
  mutate(
    FlankerEffect = str_remove(FlankerEffect, "ms"),
    FlankerEffect = as.numeric(FlankerEffect),
    FlankerEffect = if_else(
      abs(FlankerEffect) > 500,
      NA_real_,
      FlankerEffect
    )
  )

study_raw %>%
  summarise(
    mean_flanker = mean(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_flanker = sd(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_flanker = min(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_flanker = max(FlankerEffect, na.rm = TRUE),
    missing_flanker = sum(is.na(FlankerEffect))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The Flanker Effect variable was cleaned before creating the analysis dataset. Some values were imported with the text `"ms"` attached, so this text was removed and the variable was converted to numeric. Extremely large Flanker Effect values were then recoded as missing because they were outside the plausible range for this task and would disproportionately influence the mean, standard deviation, ANOVA results, and figure. After cleaning, the usable Flanker Effect scores ranged from -162 to 199, with 38 missing values. This means the Flanker analyses use only participants with valid, interpretable Flanker Effect scores.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: create_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_vars <- c(
  
      "gender", "age", "condition",
      
      "Compatible Score",
      "Incongruent Score",
      "FlankerEffect",

      "emo3", "emo8", "emo12", "emo18",
      "seek2", "seek7", "seek11", "seek17",
      "sens5", "sens10", "sens15", "sens20",
      "soc1", "soc6", "soc13", "soc16",
      "mood4", "mood9", "mood14", "mood19",

      "pos1", "pos3", "pos5", "pos9", "pos10",
      "pos14", "pos16", "pos17", "pos19",

      "neg2", "neg4", "neg6", "neg7", "neg8", "neg11", 
      "neg12", "neg13", "neg15", "neg18", "neg20"

      )

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(
    across(everything(), as.numeric)
  )
```

### Check Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

length(study_vars)

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
```

The output confirms that the final analysis dataset contains the variables required for the study, including condition assignment, Flanker Task scores, PANAS items, BMRQ items, and participant identifiers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Variable Preparation

Several variables were prepared before analysis. PANAS and BMRQ items were scored to create composite measures of positive affect, negative affect, and music reward sensitivity. Variables were recoded as needed, and a median split was used to classify participants into high and low music reward sensitivity groups for the 2 × 2 ANOVA analyses.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

```{r}
#| label: prepare_id_and_condition
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Control", "Arousal")
    ))
```

The output confirms that a unique participant identifier and the experimental condition variable were successfully added to the analysis dataset.

### Check Experimental Condition Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_condition_and_sex_coding
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n")
  )

```

The output confirms that the experimental condition variable was coded correctly into the Control and Arousal groups. Participants were distributed evenly across conditions (n = 47 per group), with a small number of cases containing missing condition data that were handled during subsequent analyses.

### Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_prepared_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
```

The output confirms that the analysis dataset was successfully prepared and contains 99 participants and 47 variables. The dataset includes the key variables required for analysis, including experimental condition, Flanker Task performance, PANAS items, BMRQ items, and demographic variables.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Reverse-Code Scale Items

Reverse-coded BMRQ items were recoded so that higher values consistently reflected greater music reward sensitivity across all questionnaire items.

```{r}
#| label: reverse_code_items
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    
        seek2r = 6 - seek2,
        sens5r = 6 - sens5
    
  )
```

### Check Reverse Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_reverse_coding
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(seek2, seek2r, sens5, sens5r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create Scale Scores

Composite scale scores were created for the BMRQ and PANAS measures by averaging responses across their respective items. These scores provide overall measures of music reward sensitivity, positive affect, and negative affect that were used in subsequent reliability analyses and ANOVAs.

```{r}
#| label: create_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

bmrq_items <- c(
            "emo3", "emo8", "emo12", "emo18", "seek2r", "seek7", 
            "seek11", "seek17", "sens5r", "sens10", "sens15", 
            "sens20", "soc1", "soc6", "soc13", "soc16","mood4", 
            "mood9", "mood14", "mood19"
          )

positive_affect_items <- c(
            "pos1", "pos3", "pos5", "pos9", "pos10", 
            "pos14", "pos16", "pos17", "pos19"
          )

negative_affect_items <- c(
            "neg2", "neg4", "neg6", "neg7", "neg8", 
            "neg11", "neg12", "neg13", "neg15", "neg18", "neg20"
        )


study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    bmrq_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(bmrq_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    positive_affect = rowMeans(across(all_of(positive_affect_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    negative_affect = rowMeans(across(all_of(negative_affect_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

### Check Scale Scores

```{r}
#| label: check_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(
    bmrq_scale,
    positive_affect,
    negative_affect
  ) %>%
  summary()

study_data %>%
  summarise(
    bmrq_scale_missing = mean(is.na(bmrq_scale)),
    postive_affect_missing = mean(is.na(positive_affect)),
    negative_affect_missing = mean(is.na(negative_affect))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The summary statistics indicate that the composite scale scores were successfully created and fall within the expected range for each measure. These results suggest that the scoring procedures were implemented correctly and that the variables are suitable for further analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create BMRQ Median Split

```{r}
#| label: create_median_split_groups
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

bmrq_median <- median(study_data$bmrq_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

study_data <- study_data %>%
mutate(
    bmrq_msplit = if_else(bmrq_scale <= bmrq_median, "Low", "High"),
    bmrq_msplit = factor(bmrq_msplit, levels = c("Low", "High"))
    )

bmrq_median

study_data %>%
  count(bmrq_msplit) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Commitment Group", "n")
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Check Scale Reliability

Reliability analyses were conducted to evaluate the internal consistency of the BMRQ and PANAS scales. Examining reliability helps determine whether the items within each measure consistently assess the same underlying construct before creating final composite scores for analysis.

```{r}
#| label: check_reliability
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

bmrq_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(bmrq_items))
positive_affect_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(positive_affect_items))
negative_affect_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(negative_affect_items))

bmrq_alpha <- psych::alpha(bmrq_item_df)
bmrq_omega <- psych::omega(bmrq_item_df, plot = FALSE)

positive_affect_alpha <- psych::alpha(positive_affect_item_df)
positive_affect_omega <- psych::omega(positive_affect_item_df, plot = FALSE)

negative_affect_alpha <- psych::alpha(negative_affect_item_df)
negative_affect_omega <- psych::omega(negative_affect_item_df, plot = FALSE)

reliability_table <- tibble(
  scale = c("Music Sensitivity", "Positive Affect", "Negative Affect"),
  alpha = c(
    bmrq_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    positive_affect_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    negative_affect_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    bmrq_omega$omega.tot,
    positive_affect_omega$omega.tot,
    negative_affect_omega$omega.tot
  )
)

reliability_table %>%
  mutate(
    alpha = round(alpha, 2),
    omega = round(omega, 2)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Scale", "Cronbach's alpha", "McDonald's omega")
  )
```

Reliability analyses indicated excellent internal consistency for the BMRQ (α = .91, ω = .94) and PANAS (α = .89, ω = .92). These findings support the use of the composite scale scores in subsequent analyses.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics were calculated to summarize the distributions of the primary study variables before conducting inferential analyses.

### Scale Score Descriptives

```{r}
#| label: scale_score_descriptives
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Descriptive statistics for continuous scale variables
study_data %>%
  select(bmrq_scale, positive_affect, negative_affect, FlankerEffect) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
```

The descriptive statistics summarize the distributions of the primary study variables. Participants reported moderate levels of music reward sensitivity and positive affect, relatively low levels of negative affect, and substantial variability in Flanker Effect scores. These values provide an overview of the sample prior to hypothesis testing and indicate that the variables were successfully computed.

```{r}
#| label: sample_composition_demographic_variables
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  summarise(
    mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    sd_age = round(sd(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    min_age = min(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_age = max(age, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Mean Age", "SD Age", "Minimum Age", "Maximum Age")
  )

study_data %>%
  mutate(
    gender = factor(gender,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
      labels = c(
        "Male",
        "Female",
        "Non-binary / third gender",
        "Prefer not to say"
      )
    )
  )

study_data %>%
 count(gender) %>%
 mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
 knitr::kable()
```

The demographic characteristics describe the composition of the sample. Participants ranged in age from young adulthood to older adulthood, providing a relatively broad age range. The gender distribution indicates the representation of participants across gender categories and provides context for interpreting the study findings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Correlations Among the DVs

Pearson correlations were conducted to examine the relationships among the primary dependent variables, including Flanker Effect scores, positive affect, and negative affect. Examining these associations provides insight into whether affective states were related to selective attention performance.

```{r}
#| label: correlation_test
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Correlations for the main continuous dependent variables
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% 
    select(
      FlankerEffect,
      positive_affect,
      negative_affect
    )
)
```

Positive affect and negative affect were significantly positively correlated, indicating that participants who reported higher levels of positive affect also tended to report higher levels of negative affect. Flanker Effect scores were not significantly associated with either affect measure, suggesting that selective attention performance was largely unrelated to self-reported affective state in the current sample.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Primary Analyses

Three separate 2 × 2 ANOVAs were conducted to examine the effects of music condition (Arousal vs. Control) and music reward sensitivity (High vs. Low) on Flanker Effect scores, positive affect, and negative affect. These analyses were used to evaluate whether the music prime influenced cognitive performance and affective state and whether these effects differed as a function of music reward sensitivity.

### Flanker Attention Model

```{r}
#| label: anova_flanker_bmrq
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a1_flanker <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "FlankerEffect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_flanker
```

### Estimated Means for Flanker Attention

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_trait_condition_sex
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition)

emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ bmrq_msplit)

emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Positive Mood Model

```{r}
#| label: anova_positive_mood
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a1_positive_affect <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "positive_affect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_positive_affect
```

### Estimated Means for Positive Mood

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_positive_mood
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition)

emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ bmrq_msplit)

emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Negative Mood Model

```{r}
#| label: anova_negative_mood
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a1_negative_affect <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "negative_affect",
  between = c("condition", "bmrq_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_negative_affect
```

### Estimated Means for Negative Mood

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_trait_condition_commitment
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition)

emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ bmrq_msplit)

emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Figures

The figure below displays mean Flanker Effect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.Flanker Attention Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: flanker_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_flanker, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    color = bmrq_msplit,
    group = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    size = 3
  ) +
  geom_line(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.35),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Flanker Effect",
    color = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(-50, 100, 25)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(-50, 100)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar
```

Participants in the music condition tended to exhibit larger Flanker Effect scores than participants in the control condition, regardless of music reward sensitivity level. The relatively overlapping confidence intervals suggest that these differences were small, consistent with the nonsignificant ANOVA findings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Positive Affect Interaction Plot

The figure below displays mean positive affect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

```{r}
#| label: positive_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_positive_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Positive Affect",
    fill = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar
```

The Positive Affect figure shows a slight tendency for participants in the music condition to report higher positive affect than participants in the control condition. However, positive affect scores were generally similar across groups, and the overlapping confidence intervals are consistent with the absence of significant main or interaction effects.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Negative Affect Interaction Plot

The figure below displays mean negative affect scores across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

```{r}
#| label: negative_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_negative_affect, ~ condition * bmrq_msplit)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = bmrq_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Condition",
    y = "Mean Negative Affect",
    fill = "BMRQ Group"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Low" = "#E76F51",
      "High" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar
```

Negative affect scores remained relatively stable across conditions and music reward sensitivity groups. The substantial overlap in confidence intervals indicates minimal differences between groups, supporting the nonsignificant ANOVA results.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Export Figures

```{r}
#| label: save_figures
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/flanker_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_flanker_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/positive_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_positive_affect_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/negative_affect_condition_bmrq_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_negative_affect_condition_bmrq_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)
```

The figures illustrate the pattern of means across music conditions and music reward sensitivity groups for each dependent variable. Participants in the music condition tended to show slightly larger Flanker Effect scores and slightly higher positive affect than participants in the control condition, whereas negative affect remained relatively similar across groups. Overall, the figures suggest minimal differences between experimental conditions, consistent with the statistical results.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Results Summary

The present study examined whether a brief music prime influenced selective attention and affective state and whether these effects depended on music reward sensitivity. Overall, the findings did not support the study hypotheses. Participants exposed to the music prime did not demonstrate significantly different levels of selective attention, positive affect, or negative affect compared to participants in the control condition. Additionally, music reward sensitivity did not appear to influence the relationship between the music prime and the outcome variables. Although some descriptive differences were observed between conditions, the overall pattern of results suggests that the brief music prime had limited effects on cognitive performance and affective state in the current sample.

## Reproducibility Information

The information below describes the software environment used to create this report. Recording the R version, operating system, and package versions helps ensure that the analyses can be reproduced and verified by other researchers.

```{r}
#| label: session_info
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

sessionInfo()
```
